statusLine: EU fuel reserves are under strain in March 2026, with 15 of 27 member states recording at least one fuel category below the mandatory 90-day minimum, and jet fuel emerging as the bloc's most acute vulnerability.
fullAnalysis:
As of March 2026, EU-wide average stock levels stand at 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87 days for jet fuel — meaning diesel and jet fuel averages are both below the mandatory 90-day minimum for net imports. While the bloc-wide figures suggest a situation still within manageable range, these averages mask severe disparities at the country level. Fifteen of 27 member states carry at least one fuel type rated critical, and the recent drawdown of strategic reserves in response to Middle East supply disruptions has left buffers thinner than headline numbers imply.

Jet fuel is the most widespread pressure point across the bloc. Twenty of 27 countries register jet fuel stocks at critical status, including major aviation economies such as France (65 days), Germany (59.9 days), Italy (61.5 days), and the Netherlands (63.2 days). Portugal and Poland are at particularly acute levels, with 21.2 and 22.5 days respectively — well under a quarter of the required minimum. Only Ireland, Estonia, Slovenia, Croatia, and Sweden hold jet fuel stocks above or near the mandatory threshold. This pattern suggests that aviation supply chains are bearing a disproportionate share of the strain from recent reserve releases.

Diesel stocks present a similarly uneven picture. Romania holds only 30.2 days of diesel — the lowest in the dataset — while Slovakia (33.5 days), Austria (55.2 days), and Greece (52.7 days) are also critically low. By contrast, Finland (197.7 days), the Netherlands (207.5 days), and Latvia (104.8 days) maintain comfortable diesel buffers. Petrol is comparatively stronger at the EU average level, but Luxembourg (38 days), Romania (40.4 days), and Lithuania (47 days) remain critical. The geographic clustering of deficits in Central and Southern Europe warrants particular attention from supply chain planners.

On the price side, EU average petrol stands at €1.771/L and diesel at €1.781/L as of mid-June 2026. Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February, from $72.48 to $80.59 per barrel, reflecting ongoing market sensitivity to geopolitical developments. Prices vary sharply by member state: Danish motorists pay €2.392/L for petrol while Maltese consumers pay €1.34/L — a gap exceeding €1 per litre, reflecting divergent tax structures as well as supply conditions. Further crude price increases could compound pressure on countries already drawing down strategic reserves, leaving less room to absorb future shocks.
keyPoints: 15 of 27 EU member states have at least one fuel category at critical status, with diesel and jet fuel both below the EU-wide 90-day mandatory minimum (88.1 and 87 days respectively).

Jet fuel is the bloc's most acute vulnerability: 20 of 27 countries are at critical status, including Portugal (21.2 days), Poland (22.5 days), and Austria (31.9 days) — far below the 90-day threshold.
Romania holds the lowest diesel stocks in the dataset at 30.2 days, followed by Slovakia at 33.5 days; both are critically below the mandatory minimum.
Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February 2026 to $80.59/barrel, tightening cost conditions at a time when several member states have already released strategic reserves.

A significant geographic divide is evident: Baltic and Nordic states (Estonia, Latvia, Finland, Ireland) generally maintain comfortable multi-fuel buffers, while Central, Southern, and Western European states face the most concentrated pressure.
